Try HARD to REFUTE this lever. Default refuted=true if uncertain. Read /home/polaris/polaris_project/SOTA_BRIEF_V2.md, the artifacts at /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board, and the grader source if needed.

LEVER: Scope-and-constraint block + non-journal source filter (L5)
RUBRIC CRITERION CLAIMED: Exclusive Citation of 'High-Quality Journal Articles' :: Verifies that all cited sources are academic, peer-reviewed journal articles... and that other forms of literature (e.g., books, conference proceedings, news articles, blogs, non-peer-reviewed reports) are not cited (instruction-following w=0.15); also S1's 'clear introduction defining the review's scope, purpose, and organizational map' and Integration of 'AI as a Key Driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution' Theme (w=0.15) [instruction-following (gap +0.115) + readability S1]
CHANGE: (a) Opening scope block in reviewer voice, bodhi-style: define 'restructuring' in enumerated dimensions (mirrors the comprehensiveness Breadth criterion's own list: job creation, displacement, transformation, skill demands, wage effects, productivity), state the evidence rule ('This review cites only peer-reviewed, English-language journal articles; where the record is thinner, claims are limited accordingly'), and give the organizational map. Constraint becomes checkable behavior in the judge's first 100 words. (b) Source filter: DELETE class-F sentences whose only [n] resolves to vendor/blog/consultancy tier — the measured offenders ([55] Toptal, [99] amplifai, [40] student blog, [91] apporya, WEF/IDC/PwC/Goldman forecasts), ~15-25 sentences; multiset validator permits deletions from the audited drop-list only. Run BEFORE L3 so attribution never names a blog.
CLAIMED: instruction-following 0.4409 -> ~0.52 = +0.020 overall
ADMITTED FAILURE MODE: The scope block promises what the body must then keep — if any vendor-register sentence survives the filter, the block converts a silent violation into perjury, worse than saying nothing. Also thinning risk: corpus is only 17 T1 + 12 T2 of 105 sources; over-aggressive filtering guts comprehensiveness. Mitigation: filter by register-breaking pattern list, not by wholesale tier; measure word loss <8%
FAITHFULNESS CONTRACT: REVISED CONTRACT — from hedged gloss to contained adjudication. The old contract's guarantee ('worst case is a hedged, uncited, capped, contradiction-screened relational gloss') aimed at the wrong genre: the rubric pays for ADJUDICATION ('areas of consensus, debate, or uncertainty... rather than merely cataloging effects'), and mandatory hedge lexemes plus an 8% budget structurally forbid it. The revision keeps every rule that prevents fabrication and deletes only the rules that enforce timidity. CLASS F (unchanged): span-verified, byte-identical; the multiset validator now permits DELETIONS from an explicit audited drop-list (L1 debris, L5 non-journal filter, L7 dedup) — deletion cannot fabricate; mutation remains impossible. CLASS F+A (new): a FACT sentence with attribution injected, where every injected author/venue/year string must be a byte-substring of the bibliography.json or Crossref record keyed by that sentence's own [n]; validator-enforced, so a fabricated attribution cannot pass a string check. CLASS S (unchanged) plus claim-first heading check (finite verb; still no digit, no citation, no new proper noun). CLASS I (redesigned): a sentence is legal iff (1) NO digit, %, unit, or spelled-out quantity — unchanged, absolute; (2) NO citation marker, NO quotation mark — unchanged; (3) NO new proper noun, NO un-named attribution — unchanged; (4-REVISED) it instantiates exactly one TYPED MOVE with a visible label — **Interpretation**, **Mechanism link**, **Implication**, **Reconciliation**, or [Analytical synthesis] — the label IS the containment: it tells the judge (and any reader) where evidence ends and argument begins, which is cellcog's exact mechanism for unhedged original claims without fabrication. Hedge lexemes are permitted, never required. Adjudicative predicates ('depends on', 'is better read as', 'situates X as', 'reconciles with... if') are legal. Still banned: universals, 'will'/forecasts, and bare EMPIRICAL-OUTCOME assertions (a study-referent subject + outcome verb + direction: 'employment fell') — an adjudication rules on how findings RELATE; it may not mint a new finding; (5) premise adjacency unchanged: >=2 FACT sentences, >=2 distinct sources, deictic pointer, content-stem anchor — plus a new VACUITY check: a Reconciliation must share content stems with BOTH premises; (6-REVISED) budget I_BUDGET_FRAC 0.08 -> 0.35, <=1 labeled move per paragraph, plus one dedicated Critical Synthesis section of reconciliation triplets; (7) fail-closed contradiction screen unchanged: CONTRADICTED, judge error, or sentinel => dropped, never kept. GUARANTEE (revised): the worst content that can ship is a labeled, digit-free, entity-free, premise-anchored, contradiction-screened relational ruling over already-verified facts. It can be wrong in EMPHASIS — which is the licensed, labeled genre of a literature review — but it cannot state a fact, number, quote, or attribution that is not already verified, because those are deterministic string checks, not model discipline. No fabricated fact/number/entity ships, ever; any validator breach reverts the paragraph, any structural breach reverts the document.

ATTACK THROUGH THIS LENS:
THE PRIOR NEGATIVE + NOISE: our one previous "insight directive" A/B scored 0.4094 vs a 0.4447 control — it made things WORSE. Is this genuinely different, or the same idea in new clothes? And is the claimed gain above the +0.0094 resolvable floor?

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

2	# (Every number here is MEASURED from primary artifacts on disk or read from the grader's own source code.)

5	| system | OVERALL | compreh | INSIGHT | instr-follow | readability | words | H3 | med-para | bullets |

17	(cellcog-max = 0.5578 corpus-wide #1; it publishes no per-task breakdown. Its task-72 artifact: 16,334w, 31 H3, 75w paras, 0 bullets.)
18	**"ADORE 52.65" IS FICTION** — absent from the backing data of both boards. Do not plan against it.

20	## 2. THE GAP, DECOMPOSED (us -> bodhi)
21	| dim | gap | weight | overall |
22	|---|---|---|---|
23	| INSIGHT | +0.122 | 0.32 | **+0.039** |
24	| instruction-following | +0.115 | 0.25 | +0.029 |
25	| comprehensiveness | +0.089 | 0.29 | +0.026 |
26	| readability | **+0.143** | 0.14 | +0.020 |
27	| | | | **= +0.114 -> 0.552** |
28	**OUR READABILITY (0.3774) IS THE WORST SCORE ON THE ENTIRE BOARD** — below grok and perplexity, the two systems we beat.
29	
30	## 3. HOW THE GRADER WORKS (read from its source, not inferred)
31	- **ONE LLM call scores BOTH reports SIDE BY SIDE.** Ours = article_1, reference = article_2, always. (race.py:99-104)
32	- **0-10 per criterion**, ~25 criteria. Bands: 4-6 "Average", **6-8 "Good"**, **8-10 "Excellent"**.
33	  We are being read at ~6.6; the reference reads ~9.0. **To beat parity a criterion must cross from "Good" into "Excellent". There is no credit for being a better 7.**
34	- **Overall = ours/(ours+reference)** — COMPRESSIVE. +1.0 raw point on EVERY criterion = only **+0.036 overall**.
35	- **THE JUDGE WRITES A COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS BEFORE IT SCORES** (forced chain-of-thought, prompt lines 47-75), and
36	  **ALL ~25 CRITERIA ACROSS ALL 4 DIMENSIONS ARE SCORED IN ONE CALL, ONE CONTEXT.** Nothing firewalls the dimensions.
37	  **=> CROSS-DIMENSION BLEED IS REAL AND UNINSTRUCTED.** The narrative it writes about our 677-word walls is still in
38	  context when it scores our insight. Our readability disaster is taxing the other 86% of the score.
39	- **Dimension weights (100-task mean): insight 0.352 | comprehensiveness 0.292 | instruction-following 0.215 | readability 0.141.**
40	  Insight + comprehensiveness = **64% of the score**.
41	- **LENGTH IS NEVER MENTIONED IN THE SCORING PROMPT.** No criterion rewards it. Padding is penalised at only ~0.7% of
42	  overall, while substance-bearing expansion pays into 64%. => "longer but DENSER" wins; "longer and thinner" is pointless.
43	- **EXECUTIVE SUMMARIES ARE NOT REWARDED.** Proof: the reference ships an EMPTY PLACEHOLDER exec summary
44	  ("this section will be written after the report body is complete") and still beats every system on every task.
45	- **HEDGING IS NOT REWARDED.** No criterion anywhere pays for softness or uncertainty-as-stance. What IS rewarded (51
46	  criteria, inside the 64%-weight dimensions) is **CRITICAL SCRUTINY**: "Critical Evaluation of Evidence and Synthesis of
47	  Competing Theories", "Critical Scrutiny of Limitations". **The judge does not want us to sound uncertain. It wants us to ADJUDICATE.**
48	- INSIGHT rubric, verbatim: "deep analysis and original insights rather than simply repeating known information";
49	  "clear logical reasoning and effectively explains causal relationships behind phenomena"; "identifies key issues ...
50	  provides insightful solutions"; "forward-looking thinking, can anticipate trends".
51	- COMPREHENSIVENESS rubric: breadth AND depth AND **evidence** AND "multiple perspectives and balance" — NOT "more sections".
52	- Structure: "Overall Article Structure and Logical Flow" is the largest readability criterion and explicitly names
53	  "distinct heading levels" / "effective headings and subheadings". Tables/charts: max ~2.8% of overall. Worth having, not worth optimising hard.
54	- **RACE STRIPS EVERY CITATION MARKER AND THE ENTIRE BIBLIOGRAPHY BEFORE JUDGING** (utils/clean_article.py, an LLM
55	  cleaner). VERIFIED: our 9,300 words -> 7,692 read; 345 [n] markers -> 0; 105-entry bibliography -> DELETED.
56	  **=> ALL bibliography/citation-quality work scores ZERO. Source quality can ONLY be signalled by NAMING VENUES/AUTHORS
57	  IN THE RUNNING PROSE.** (The 0.5102 reference ships ZERO inline citations.)
58	
59	## 4. WHAT THE WINNERS DO (measured from their task-72 artifacts)
60	- **5 of the 6 systems above the reference have 31-50 H3 subsections. Every system at/below the reference has ZERO.**
61	  (dalpha is the lone exception: 0 H3, but 38-word paragraphs.) **We have 0 H3 and 677-word paragraphs.**
62	- Winners' median paragraph: **59-75 words**. Reference: 131. OpenAI: 170. **Us: 677 — a different regime entirely.**
63	- **LENGTH IS DEAD AS A LEVER: bodhi WINS TASK 72 ON 4,361 WORDS — 44% SHORTER THAN OURS.** dalpha scores 0.5252 on 4,039.
64	- Bullets are NOT the lever (sourcery ships 106 and beats the reference; cellcog ships 0). Tables are NOT the lever (0-5).
65	- cellcog's synthesis pattern (measured in its prose): grounded finding A (named study + number) -> grounded finding B
66	  (a CONFLICTING named study) -> a reconciliation sentence carrying NO new facts -> enumerated candidate explanations ->
67	  an explicit statement of what the evidence CANNOT yet resolve. Plus a dedicated "Critical Synthesis" section
68	  (convergence / outstanding tensions / limitations / future research).
69	- Winners ADAPT SHAPE TO GENRE (bullet-density varies 3-10x between applied and scholarly tasks); losers apply one template.
70	
71	## 5. WHAT POLARIS IS (the constraint you must design around)
72	- An extraction-grounded FACT CONVEYOR. `verify_sentence_provenance` (provenance_generator.py:2098) admits a sentence
73	  only if `len(failures)==0`; the entailment judge's NEUTRAL clause (entailment_judge.py:588) kills any sentence that
74	  "introduces a fact, entity, **MECHANISM** ... NOT present in the SPAN".
75	- **A cross-source inference is, BY DEFINITION, a mechanism not present in either span. Our own gate deletes exactly the
76	  sentence type that earns INSIGHT (0.32 — the heaviest weight, and our worst dimension).**
77	- Boundary, precisely: a CONJUNCTION of two sources ("X found 2.1% [a] while Y found 0.4% [b]") PASSES. Add one
78	  interpretive clause ("a gap that may reflect slower adoption") and it is KILLED.
79	- **FAITHFULNESS IS THE MOAT AND IS NOT FOR SALE.** No fabricated fact, number, quote or attribution may ever ship. Any
80	  design that can leak one is unshippable, whatever it scores. We have a working deterministic contract
81	  (scripts/reflow_report.py): FACT (span-verified, byte-identical) / STRUCTURE (assembled from sidecar data) /
82	  INTERPRETATION (no digits, no new proper nouns, no citations, must sit beside >=2 facts from >=2 sources,
83	  contradiction-screened fail-closed). **NOTE: it was built around HEDGING — and the rubric does not reward hedging.
84	  It rewards ADJUDICATION. That contract needs rethinking, not just retuning.**
85	
86	## 6. MEASUREMENT (fixed tonight — use it, never score n=1 again)
87	- Judge noise: **SD = 0.0074** (byte-identical artifact scored 6x). Baseline is tighter (SD 0.0020).
88	- k=5 paired => SE(diff) = 0.0047 => **smallest resolvable effect = +0.0094 at 2 sigma.**
89	- Generation noise (re-composing the same config) is LARGER (~0.016) and applies ONLY to arms needing a new compose.
90	  A report->report transform of a BANKED artifact has ZERO generation noise — cheap AND sensitive. Prefer it.
91	- Our own history: baseline 0.4062 -> rank10 0.4382 = **+0.032, z=10** (real). The depth levers DID work, modestly.
92	  But every subsequent lever (padding removal, source firewall) scored FLAT. And ALL of it was aimed at proxies.
93	
94	## 7. YOUR TASK
95	Design the plan that takes POLARIS from **0.4382** to **the top of this board (0.54-0.56)**.
96	- Ground every lever in the rubric text or a measured artifact. NO PROXIES — we lost a night to them.
97	- State, for each lever: the criterion it moves, the expected points, how it FAILS, and the cheap test that proves the
98	  mechanism fired BEFORE we pay for a 65-minute compose.
99	- Respect the faithfulness moat absolutely. If a lever needs ungrounded prose, say so explicitly and justify the exact
100	  containment.
101	- Be honest about the ceiling. If your plan does not reach 0.54, SAY SO and name the residual.
102	- Where you are UNCERTAIN, SEARCH (papers, GitHub, the artifacts on disk at
103	  /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board/). Do not guess.

27:- [w=0.15] Exclusive Citation of 'High-Quality Journal Articles' :: Verifies that all cited sources are academic, peer-reviewed journal articles (or meet a clear standard of 'high-quality' if defined contextually, e.g., leading journals), and that other forms of literature (e.g., books, conference proceedings, news articles, blogs, non-peer-reviewed reports) are not cited, adhering to the 'only cites high-quality... journal articles' constraint.
28:- [w=0.10] Exclusive Citation of 'English-Language' Journal Articles :: Ensures that all journal articles cited in the review are originally published in English or are established English translations, strictly adhering to the language constraint for sources.
29-
30-=== READABILITY (weight 0.14) ===
31-- [w=0.20] L1: Language Clarity, Precision, and Academic Tone :: Evaluates the accuracy and fluency of sentence construction, grammar, spelling, and punctuation. Assesses the precise, consistent, and appropriate use of technical terminology related to AI, labor markets, the Fourth Industrial Revolution, and specific industries. Ensures an objective, formal academic tone suitable for a literature review. For example, terms like 'skill-biased technical change' or specific AI techniques should be used correctly and, if necessary, briefly contextualized for a broader academic audience.
32-- [w=0.20] S1: Overall Structure and Logical Organization :: Assesses the coherence and logic of the review's macro-structure. This includes a clear introduction defining the review's scope, purpose, and organizational map; logically sequenced thematic sections with informative headings that reflect the facets of AI's impact on labor (e.g., by industry, skill level, type of disruption); and a conclusion that effectively synthesizes the main themes from the literature and perhaps points to gaps or future research directions.
33-- [w=0.15] S2: Paragraph Cohesion and Transitions :: Evaluates if each paragraph focuses on a single, clear idea or theme derived from the literature, with sentences flowing logically within the paragraph. Assesses the effectiveness of transitional phrases, sentences, and logical connectors in linking ideas smoothly between paragraphs and across different sections of the review, ensuring a coherent narrative thread.
34-- [w=0.15] P1: Clarity and Synthesis in Presenting Sourced Information :: Assesses how clearly, concisely, and accurately key arguments, findings, theories, and methodologies from the cited journal articles are presented. Evaluates the effectiveness of synthesizing information from multiple sources to build a coherent understanding of topics (e.g., AI's effect on job displacement vs. creation, or changes in skill demands across industries), rather than just serially summarizing individual papers. Checks for appropriate information density and avoidance of redundancy.
=====
36:- [w=0.10] F1: Formatting, Layout, and Visual Consistency :: Evaluates the use of consistent and professional formatting (e.g., font choice and size, spacing, margins, heading styles) that enhances readability. Assesses the overall visual layout for ease of navigation and reduced reader fatigue, including clear paragraph breaks. Also considers the visual consistency and unobtrusive integration of in-text citations and the clarity of the bibliography's formatting, which contributes to the academic presentation.

48	    def _clean_text(self, text, language="zh", max_retries=None):
49	        """Try to clean text. Returns string (incl. "") on success,
50	        self._TRUNCATED on output overflow, or None after all retries.
51	        max_retries kept for signature compatibility but the canonical budget
52	        is _RETRY_BACKOFFS_S (3 attempts with exponential backoff).
53	        """
54	        clean_prompt = self._get_clean_prompt(language)
55	        user_prompt = clean_prompt.format(article=text)
56	        backoffs = self._RETRY_BACKOFFS_S
57	
58	        last_err = None
59	        for attempt, sleep_s in enumerate([0, *backoffs[:-1]]):
60	            if sleep_s:
61	                time.sleep(sleep_s)
62	            try:
63	                with _llm_semaphore:
64	                    result, stop_reason = self.clean_agent.generate(
65	                        user_prompt=user_prompt, system_prompt="",
66	                        return_metadata=True, stage="clean",
67	                    )
68	                if stop_reason in ("max_tokens", "length"):
69	                    logger.warning(f"Output truncated (stop_reason={stop_reason}), needs chunking")
70	                    return self._TRUNCATED
71	                # Accept any string (including "") — empty means the chunk
72	                # was entirely a reference list, per prompt contract.
73	                if result is not None:
74	                    return result
75	                logger.warning(f"Got None from API, attempt {attempt+1}/{len(backoffs)}")
76	            except Exception as e:
77	                last_err = e
78	                logger.error(f"API call error (attempt {attempt+1}/{len(backoffs)}): {e}")
79	                if self._is_token_limit_error(e):
80	                    logger.info("Article too long, needs chunking")
81	                    return self._TRUNCATED
82	
83	        logger.error(f"Cleaning failed after {len(backoffs)} attempts; last error: {last_err}")
84	        return None

87	    def _split_at_boundary(text, target_pos):
88	        """Find nearest sentence boundary to target_pos."""
89	        search_start = max(0, target_pos - 200)
90	        for j in range(target_pos, search_start, -1):
91	            if j < len(text) and text[j] in '.?!。？！\n':
92	                return j + 1

95	    # Pre-chunking thresholds (tokens). One chunk per 50k tokens; <=50k stays whole.
96	    # 50k leaves headroom under GPT-5.5's output cap for reasoning + cleaned text.
97	    _CHUNK_TOKEN_STEP = 50_000
98	    _SPLIT_SEARCH_RADIUS = 5000
99	
100	    # Per-call retry budget when API throws (excluding fatal errors that signal
101	    # truncation or token limit, which short-circuit to recursive chunking).
102	    _RETRY_BACKOFFS_S = (2, 5, 15)

104	    @staticmethod
105	    def _estimate_tokens(text, language):
106	        """Cheap upper-bound token estimate.
107	        zh: 1 char ≈ 1 token (slight over-est, safer).
108	        en: 1 token ≈ 3.5 chars.
109	        """
110	        if not text:
111	            return 0
112	        if language == "zh":
113	            return len(text)
114	        return int(len(text) / 3.5) + 1

116	    @classmethod
117	    def _compute_num_chunks(cls, num_tokens):
118	        """Boundaries at 100k, 200k, 300k, ...
119	        <=100k → 1, (100k,200k] → 2, (200k,300k] → 3, ...
120	        """
121	        if num_tokens <= cls._CHUNK_TOKEN_STEP:
122	            return 1
123	        # ceil(num_tokens / step)
124	        return (num_tokens + cls._CHUNK_TOKEN_STEP - 1) // cls._CHUNK_TOKEN_STEP

126	    @classmethod
127	    def _find_double_newline_split(cls, text, target_pos, radius=None):
128	        """Find split position nearest to target_pos.
129	        Prefers '\\n\\n' (split AFTER the blank line); falls back to '\\n';
130	        last resort: hard cut at target_pos.
131	        """
132	        if radius is None:
133	            radius = cls._SPLIT_SEARCH_RADIUS
134	        n = len(text)
135	        lo = max(0, target_pos - radius)
136	        hi = min(n, target_pos + radius)
137	
138	        best_pos, best_dist = None, None
139	        scan = lo
140	        while scan < hi:
141	            idx = text.find("\n\n", scan, hi)
142	            if idx == -1:
143	                break
144	            dist = abs(idx - target_pos)
145	            if best_dist is None or dist < best_dist:
146	                best_dist, best_pos = dist, idx + 2
147	            scan = idx + 1
148	        if best_pos is not None:
149	            return best_pos
150	
151	        # Fallback: nearest single '\n'
152	        best_pos, best_dist = None, None
153	        for i in range(lo, hi):
154	            if text[i] == "\n":
155	                dist = abs(i - target_pos)
156	                if best_dist is None or dist < best_dist:
157	                    best_dist, best_pos = dist, i + 1
158	        if best_pos is not None:
159	            return best_pos
160	
161	        return target_pos

163	    @classmethod
164	    def _split_into_chunks(cls, text, num_chunks):
165	        """Split text into num_chunks pieces at \\n\\n boundaries near i/N positions."""
166	        if num_chunks <= 1 or not text:
167	            return [text]
168	        n = len(text)
169	        cuts = []
170	        for i in range(1, num_chunks):
171	            target = (n * i) // num_chunks
172	            cuts.append(cls._find_double_newline_split(text, target))
173	        # Ensure cuts are strictly increasing (paranoid: search radii could overlap)
174	        cuts = sorted(set(cuts))
175	
176	        chunks, prev = [], 0
177	        for c in cuts:
178	            if c > prev:
179	                chunks.append(text[prev:c])
180	                prev = c
181	        chunks.append(text[prev:])
182	        return [c for c in chunks if c]

184	    def _clean_one_chunk(self, idx, total, chunk, language, max_retries):
185	        """Clean a single pre-split chunk. Returns text or None on failure.
186	        LLM-call concurrency is gated by the module-level _llm_semaphore inside _clean_text.
187	        """
188	        t0 = time.time()
189	        result = self._clean_text(chunk, language, max_retries)
190	        if result == self._TRUNCATED:
191	            logger.info(f"Chunk {idx+1}/{total} still truncated, recursive split")
192	            result = self.chunk_clean_article(chunk, language)
193	        if not result:
194	            logger.error(f"Chunk {idx+1}/{total} failed to clean")
195	            return None
196	        logger.info(f"Chunk {idx+1}/{total} cleaned in {time.time()-t0:.1f}s "
197	                    f"(in={len(chunk):,} out={len(result):,} chars)")
198	        return result

200	    def _clean_chunked(self, article, num_chunks, language, max_retries):
201	        """Pre-split into num_chunks pieces, clean each in parallel, concatenate.
202	        Concurrent LLM calls across all chunks (and articles) are capped by
203	        the module-level semaphore.
204	        """
205	        chunks = self._split_into_chunks(article, num_chunks)
206	        logger.info(f"Pre-chunking into {len(chunks)} pieces "
207	                    f"(sizes: {[len(c) for c in chunks]} chars)")
208	
209	        # Submit all chunks; semaphore inside _clean_text enforces global cap.
210	        results = [None] * len(chunks)
211	        with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=len(chunks)) as ex:
212	            future_to_idx = {
213	                ex.submit(self._clean_one_chunk, i, len(chunks), c, language, max_retries): i
214	                for i, c in enumerate(chunks)
215	            }
216	            for fut in concurrent.futures.as_completed(future_to_idx):
217	                i = future_to_idx[fut]
218	                results[i] = fut.result()
219	
220	        if any(r is None for r in results):
221	            return None
222	        merged = "".join(results)
223	        return merged if self._is_valid_result(merged) else None

225	    def chunk_clean_article(self, article, language="zh", max_depth=3, _depth=0):
226	        """Recursively split and clean. If a half is truncated, split it again."""
227	        if _depth >= max_depth:
228	            logger.warning(f"Max chunk depth {max_depth} reached, giving up")
229	            return None
230	
231	        mid = self._split_at_boundary(article, len(article) // 2)
232	        halves = [article[:mid], article[mid:]]
233	        cleaned_parts = []
234	
235	        for i, half in enumerate(halves):
236	            if not half.strip():
237	                continue
238	            result = self._clean_text(half, language)
239	            if result == self._TRUNCATED:
240	                logger.info(f"depth={_depth} half={i} truncated, splitting further")
241	                result = self.chunk_clean_article(half, language, max_depth, _depth + 1)
242	            if result is None:
243	                logger.error(f"depth={_depth} half={i} failed")
244	                return None
245	            cleaned_parts.append(result)
246	
247	        merged = "".join(cleaned_parts)
248	        if self._is_valid_result(merged):
249	            return merged
250	        return None

415	        """Load items from input file"""
416	        all_items = []
417	        with open(input_file, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
418	            for line in f:

431	        # If output file exists, read already processed IDs
432	        if os.path.exists(output_file):
433	            logger.info(f"Found existing output file: {output_file}")
434	            with open(output_file, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
435	                for line in f:

443	            logger.info(f"Read {len(processed_ids)} already processed records from output file")
444	        else:

Focus on how AI, as a key driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, is causing significant disruptions and affecting various industries. Ensure the review only cites high-quality, English-language journal articles.

DIMENSION WEIGHTS: {"readability": 0.14, "insight": 0.32, "comprehensiveness": 0.29, "instruction_following": 0.25}

=== COMPREHENSIVENESS (weight 0.29) ===
- [w=0.10] Grounding in AI and the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) Context :: Assesses whether the review adequately defines AI within the context of the 4IR and explains its fundamental role as a driver of labor market restructuring. This contextual framing is crucial for understanding the scope and nature of the impacts discussed.
- [w=0.25] Breadth of Labor Market Restructuring Dimensions Covered :: Evaluates the extent to which the review addresses the multifaceted nature of AI's impact on the labor market, including job creation, job displacement, job transformation, changes in skill demands, wage effects, and productivity changes.
- [w=0.25] Scope of Industry-Specific Analysis :: Checks if the review examines the restructuring impact of AI across a diverse range of relevant industries, highlighting both common trends and sector-specific nuances. The task explicitly requires focusing on 'various industries.'
- [w=0.15] Exploration of AI's Disruptive Character and Scale :: Assesses whether the review sufficiently explores the 'significant disruptions' caused by AI, including the magnitude, speed, and transformative potential of these changes on labor market structures and dynamics.
- [w=0.15] Depth and Representativeness of Literature Synthesized :: Evaluates if the review synthesizes a broad and representative selection of current, high-quality academic literature (from the specified journal articles), covering the main research themes, findings, and ongoing debates regarding AI's impact on the labor market. This ensures the review's content is built upon a solid and comprehensive foundation of existing knowledge.
- [w=0.10] Balanced Discussion of AI's Labor Market Impacts :: Assesses whether the review presents a nuanced perspective by discussing both the challenges (e.g., displacement, skill gaps, inequality) and opportunities (e.g., new job creation, productivity gains, enhanced work quality) arising from AI's influence on the labor market.

=== INSIGHT (weight 0.32) ===
- [w=0.25] Analytical Depth in Characterizing AI-Driven Labor Market Restructuring Mechanisms :: Assesses if the review moves beyond superficial descriptions of labor market changes to deeply analyze the underlying *mechanisms* (e.g., task automation, skill augmentation, job creation/destruction dynamics, organizational adaptation) through which AI specifically reshapes job roles, skill requirements, and overall labor market structures.
- [w=0.25] Critical Synthesis and Nuanced Evaluation of AI's Disruptive Impacts Across Industries :: Evaluates the review's ability to critically synthesize diverse findings from high-quality journal articles to present a nuanced understanding of AI's 'significant disruptions.' This includes identifying patterns of impact, variations across different industries, and areas of consensus, debate, or uncertainty in the literature, rather than merely cataloging effects.
- [w=0.15] Insightful Integration of AI's Role within the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) Context :: Measures how effectively the review articulates and leverages the 4IR framework to provide a deeper, more contextualized understanding of the *nature, scale, and interconnectedness* of AI's transformative power on the labor market, rather than treating AI's impact in isolation or merely mentioning the 4IR superficially.
- [w=0.20] Identification and Articulation of Emergent Themes, Theoretical Linkages, or Novel Perspectives :: Assesses whether the review transcends simple summarization to identify and clearly articulate overarching emergent themes, significant theoretical linkages between concepts, or potentially novel perspectives derived from the synthesis of the reviewed literature on AI's labor market impact.
- [w=0.15] Value and Foresight in Delineating Implications and Future Research Agendas :: Evaluates the review's capacity to distill strategically valuable implications (e.g., for policy, education, workforce adaptation strategies) and to clearly delineate critical research gaps or promising future research agendas based on the comprehensive synthesis of the current state of knowledge.

=== INSTRUCTION_FOLLOWING (weight 0.25) ===
- [w=0.10] Adherence to 'Literature Review' Format and Purpose :: Assesses if the article is structured and executed as a literature review, meaning it synthesizes and discusses existing published research on the topic, rather than presenting new empirical findings or being solely an opinion piece.
- [w=0.20] Consistent Focus on 'Restructuring Impact of AI on the Labor Market' :: Evaluates whether the article's content remains consistently and primarily focused on AI's restructuring effects on the labor market, as per the core task instruction, without significant digressions into unrelated AI applications or general economic theory not tied to labor impacts.
- [w=0.15] Integration of 'AI as a Key Driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution' Theme :: Checks if the review explicitly incorporates and discusses AI within the specific context of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, as mandated by the task's focus requirements.
- [w=0.15] Explicit Addressal of AI-Driven 'Significant Disruptions' in the Labor Market :: Assesses whether the review specifically addresses and elaborates on the 'significant disruptions' caused by AI in the labor market, fulfilling another key focus point of the task.
- [w=0.15] Coverage of AI's Impact on 'Various Industries' :: Evaluates if the review discusses the impact of AI on the labor market across a spectrum of 'various industries,' rather than limiting its scope to a single industry or providing only a generic overview, as per the instruction.
- [w=0.15] Exclusive Citation of 'High-Quality Journal Articles' :: Verifies that all cited sources are academic, peer-reviewed journal articles (or meet a clear standard of 'high-quality' if defined contextually, e.g., leading journals), and that other forms of literature (e.g., books, conference proceedings, news articles, blogs, non-peer-reviewed reports) are not cited, adhering to the 'only cites high-quality... journal articles' constraint.
- [w=0.10] Exclusive Citation of 'English-Language' Journal Articles :: Ensures that all journal articles cited in the review are originally published in English or are established English translations, strictly adhering to the language constraint for sources.

=== READABILITY (weight 0.14) ===
